reasoning: The market probability was around 11% on August 7, 2025. In late June 2025, there was a major 'Twelve Day War' where the U.S. and Israel engaged in direct military strikes against Iranian nuclear sites. In response, Iran's parliament approved a bill to close the Strait of Hormuz, and traffic dropped by ~14-20% due to GPS jamming and security concerns. However, Iran did not actually successfully close or severely restrict the strait, nor did it execute a policy of systemic interdiction of Western ships during this peak escalation. A ceasefire was brokered by June 24 and appears to largely be holding into mid-August. An August 13 U.S. Maritime Administration (MARAD) advisory explicitly stated there 'remains no specific threat to U.S.-flagged vessels in this location,' even though routine risks of illegal boarding persist. The fact that the ultimate escalation—military strikes on Iran's nuclear program—did not prompt a full closure strongly implies the strategic disincentives (alienating Gulf neighbors, crippling their own economy) outweigh the desire to block the strait. Given that the immediate crisis has de-escalated and an actual severe restriction seems heavily deterred even in extreme scenarios, the probability of a closure by the end of 2025 is low. With 4.5 months remaining and the market anchored around 11%, I predict 9% due to the relative stability post-June.
